content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Idiyappam**. Idiyappam (also known as Putu Mayam in the Malay-Singapore context) is a delicate South Indian and Sri Lankan dish of steamed rice flour noodles. A soft dough of rice flour and water is pressed through a mould with fine perforations to create thin vermicelli-like strands, which are arranged into flat nests and steamed until tender. Each nest is white, slightly translucent, and has a soft, silky texture. It is virtually fat-free and naturally gluten-free. In Singapore, putu mayam is sold by street vendors with freshly grated coconut and palm sugar (gula melaka), and also served at South Indian restaurants with coconut milk and curry. Per 100g: 162.0 kcal, 2.2g protein, 0.5g fat, 36.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 120.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 74 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 49.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from steamed rice vermicelli; GI is high due to the refined white rice flour with minimal fibre and protein to slow digestion. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 216mg per 3-nest serving (180g) — low; idiyappam is one of the lowest-sodium cooked grain products. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients; virtually sodium-free in the idiyappam itself. Sodium intake is primarily determined by the accompaniments (curry, coconut milk). Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. High GI (~74) and high carbohydrate content from refined white rice flour; glycaemic load per 3-nest serving (180g) is approximately 49 — very high. Diabetic patients should limit to 1–2 nests and pair with high-protein accompaniments (egg, chicken stew, lentils) to moderate the glycaemic response. Ragi idiyappam (finger millet) is a lower-GI alternative. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Extremely low in fat and allergens; rice flour is non-inflammatory; the dish itself is very pure. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — gluten-free, vegan, and very low in fat. Pair with anti-inflammatory curries containing turmeric and ginger. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Per_100g values are for plain idiyappam only (no coconut milk or sugar). Accompaniments (sweetened coconut milk + palm sugar from street vendor putu mayam) would add approximately 60–80 kcal and 15–18g sugar per serving. GI is notably high due to refined rice flour — ragi (finger millet) idiyappam is a better choice for diabetic patients.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Sambar**. Sambar is the quintessential South Indian lentil and vegetable stew — a moderately thin, deeply flavoured, tangy-spicy broth made from pressure-cooked toor dal (split pigeon peas) blended with tamarind water and a robust sambar masala spice blend. It typically contains drumstick pods, brinjal, tomato, and onion, finished with a mustard seed and curry leaf tadka. Sambar is served as an essential accompaniment to idli, dosa, vada, and rice across South India and Singapore's Indian restaurants. It is vegan, gluten-free, and an excellent source of plant protein and fibre. Per 100g: 55.0 kcal, 2.8g protein, 1.5g fat, 8.5g carbohydrates, 2.0g dietary fibre, 320.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 35 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 6.0. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from toor dal (pigeon pea) GI; legume-based stew with significant fibre content gives a low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 640mg per bowl (200g) — elevated; restaurant sambar is notoriously high in salt; home-cooked sambar can be prepared with half the salt. Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt sambar or limit their sambar serving to 100–150g. Sambar made at home allows precise sodium control — target under 300mg sodium per bowl. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~35) due to toor dal and vegetable fibre; glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 6 — negligible. Sambar is highly beneficial for diabetic patients — pairing sambar with idli, dosa, or rice significantly lowers the overall glycaemic response of the meal. Encourage liberal sambar consumption. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Turmeric and tamarind have potent anti-inflammatory properties; drumstick (moringa) is exceptionally rich in iron and vitamin C; toor dal provides folate. Sambar is highly beneficial for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The turmeric content (curcumin) and drumstick (moringa) make it one of the most anti-inflammatory foods in South Indian cuisine. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Sodium is the primary health concern — restaurant sambar can contain 300–600mg sodium per 100g depending on preparation. Drumstick (moringa) pods, if included, significantly increase iron and vitamin C content. Sambar recipe varies widely by family and region.

Sambar is the quintessential South Indian lentil and vegetable stew — a moderately thin, deeply flavoured, tangy-spicy broth made from pressure-cooked toor dal (split pigeon peas) blended with tamarind water and a robust sambar masala spice blend. It typically contains drumstick pods, brinjal, tomato, and onion, finished with a mustard seed and curry leaf tadka. Sambar is served as an essential accompaniment to idli, dosa, vada, and rice across South India and Singapore's Indian restaurants. It is vegan, gluten-free, and an excellent source of plant protein and fibre. Per 100g: 55.0 kcal, 2.8g protein, 1.5g fat, 8.5g carbohydrates, 2.0g dietary fibre, 320.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 35 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 6.0. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from toor dal (pigeon pea) GI; legume-based stew with significant fibre content gives a low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 640mg per bowl (200g) — elevated; restaurant sambar is notoriously high in salt; home-cooked sambar can be prepared with half the salt. Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt sambar or limit their sambar serving to 100–150g. Sambar made at home allows precise sodium control — target under 300mg sodium per bowl. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~35) due to toor dal and vegetable fibre; glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 6 — negligible. Sambar is highly beneficial for diabetic patients — pairing sambar with idli, dosa, or rice significantly lowers the overall glycaemic response of the meal. Encourage liberal sambar consumption. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Turmeric and tamarind have potent anti-inflammatory properties; drumstick (moringa) is exceptionally rich in iron and vitamin C; toor dal provides folate. Sambar is highly beneficial for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The turmeric content (curcumin) and drumstick (moringa) make it one of the most anti-inflammatory foods in South Indian cuisine. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Sodium is the primary health concern — restaurant sambar can contain 300–600mg sodium per 100g depending on preparation. Drumstick (moringa) pods, if included, significantly increase iron and vitamin C content. Sambar recipe varies widely by family and region.

Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Rasam**. Rasam is a thin, intensely sour and peppery South Indian broth — often described as South India's answer to soup — made from tamarind extract, tomato, thin toor dal water, and a rasam masala powder heavy in black pepper, coriander, and cumin. A ghee tadka of mustard seeds, cumin, curry leaves, and garlic is added at the end. Rasam is much thinner and more acidic than sambar. It is consumed as a soup, poured over rice as part of a South Indian meal, or drunk as a digestive remedy for cold and congestion. Extremely low in calories, it is one of the most beneficial drinks in traditional South Indian food culture. Per 100g: 28.0 kcal, 0.8g protein, 0.8g fat, 4.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 285.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 570mg per bowl (200g) — elevated; rasam is traditionally quite salty and sour. Home preparation allows sodium reduction. Hypertensive patients should request reduced-salt rasam or limit to 100ml. The high potassium from tamarind and tomato provides some offsetting benefit. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Extremely low calorie and carbohydrate content — approximately 56 kcal and 9g carbohydrate per full bowl; GI testing not applicable. Rasam is ideal for diabetic patients — very low in calories and carbohydrates. Black pepper in rasam may improve insulin sensitivity. Patients can consume freely. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Black pepper (piperine), turmeric, and tamarind have potent anti-inflammatory properties; rasam is one of the most medicinal foods in South Indian cuisine. Highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Traditionally consumed for joint pain and respiratory conditions. Black pepper enhances turmeric bioavailability. Contains ghee which provides fat-soluble vitamin benefits. Note: Contains ghee (small amount) — not vegan; dairy allergen present at trace levels. Cholesterol approximately 3mg per 200g bowl from ghee. GI and GL are listed as null as rasam is a non-meaningful GI test subject — too low in carbohydrates. Sodium values may vary significantly between restaurants and home cooking (250–500mg per 100ml is typical restaurant range).

This is **Coconut Chutney**. Coconut Chutney is the indispensable fresh condiment of South Indian cuisine — a smooth, creamy white-beige paste made by grinding freshly grated coconut with roasted chana dal, green chilli, ginger, and salt, then finished with a fragrant tadka (tempering) of coconut oil, mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and curry leaves. Served in a small bowl alongside idli, dosa, vada, and uttapam. It is the cooling, rich, slightly nutty counterpart to the tangy-spicy sambar. Very fresh and perishable — typically consumed on the day of preparation. In Singapore, coconut chutney is available at all South Indian restaurants. Per 100g: 212.0 kcal, 3.2g protein, 18.5g fat, 11.5g carbohydrates, 5.2g dietary fibre, 280.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 18 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 0.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from fresh coconut flesh GI; the high fat and fibre content of fresh coconut gives a very low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 112mg per standard serving (40g) — low; the chutney provides 220mg potassium per 100g which helps offset sodium intake. Suitable for hypertensive patients at standard serving sizes. Salt can be reduced in home preparation. Fresh coconut provides potassium which is beneficial for blood pressure management. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~18); glycaemic load per 40g serving is less than 1 — negligible. High fat and fibre content of coconut slows gastric emptying. Coconut chutney is an excellent accompaniment for diabetic patients — it adds virtually no glycaemic load to a meal and the coconut fat slows absorption of the accompanying starch. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Fresh coconut contains medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) with lauric acid which has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties; green chilli and ginger are anti-inflammatory. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Fresh coconut chutney provides a beneficial lauric acid content and anti-inflammatory spice compounds. Standard serving size is small (40g) so total fat intake is modest. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Contains tree nut allergen (coconut). Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based preparation. The very high saturated fat per 100g (~15.5g) is entirely from fresh coconut — medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs), primarily lauric acid. At the standard 40g serving size, total fat is approximately 7.4g. Fresh chutney is highly perishable — must be consumed within 1 day.

Coconut Chutney is the indispensable fresh condiment of South Indian cuisine — a smooth, creamy white-beige paste made by grinding freshly grated coconut with roasted chana dal, green chilli, ginger, and salt, then finished with a fragrant tadka (tempering) of coconut oil, mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and curry leaves. Served in a small bowl alongside idli, dosa, vada, and uttapam. It is the cooling, rich, slightly nutty counterpart to the tangy-spicy sambar. Very fresh and perishable — typically consumed on the day of preparation. In Singapore, coconut chutney is available at all South Indian restaurants. Per 100g: 212.0 kcal, 3.2g protein, 18.5g fat, 11.5g carbohydrates, 5.2g dietary fibre, 280.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 18 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 0.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from fresh coconut flesh GI; the high fat and fibre content of fresh coconut gives a very low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 112mg per standard serving (40g) — low; the chutney provides 220mg potassium per 100g which helps offset sodium intake. Suitable for hypertensive patients at standard serving sizes. Salt can be reduced in home preparation. Fresh coconut provides potassium which is beneficial for blood pressure management. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~18); glycaemic load per 40g serving is less than 1 — negligible. High fat and fibre content of coconut slows gastric emptying. Coconut chutney is an excellent accompaniment for diabetic patients — it adds virtually no glycaemic load to a meal and the coconut fat slows absorption of the accompanying starch. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Fresh coconut contains medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) with lauric acid which has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties; green chilli and ginger are anti-inflammatory. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Fresh coconut chutney provides a beneficial lauric acid content and anti-inflammatory spice compounds. Standard serving size is small (40g) so total fat intake is modest. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Contains tree nut allergen (coconut). Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based preparation. The very high saturated fat per 100g (~15.5g) is entirely from fresh coconut — medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs), primarily lauric acid. At the standard 40g serving size, total fat is approximately 7.4g. Fresh chutney is highly perishable — must be consumed within 1 day.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Curd Rice**. Curd Rice (Thayir Sadam) is the ultimate South Indian comfort food — warm or room-temperature cooked white rice gently mashed and mixed with fresh plain yogurt (curd) until creamy and smooth, then finished with a hot oil tempering of mustard seeds, urad dal, dried red chilli, curry leaves, ginger, and green chilli. It is cooling, soothing, and mildly sour. Traditionally the last dish in a South Indian meal, eaten to calm the palate after spicy curries. Often garnished with pomegranate seeds and fresh coriander. Rich in probiotics from yogurt and calcium, making it one of the most digestive-friendly rice preparations. Per 100g: 122.0 kcal, 3.5g protein, 2.8g fat, 21.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 168.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 58 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 31.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from cooked white rice with full-fat yogurt; the yogurt's protein and fat significantly lower the GI compared to plain rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 420mg per bowl (250g) — moderate; the yogurt contributes natural sodium and the tempering adds salt. Calcium from yogurt supports blood pressure regulation. Suitable for hypertensive patients; the calcium from yogurt (~205mg per bowl) is beneficial for blood pressure. Reduce added salt during preparation. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~58) due to the yogurt protein and fat slowing starch digestion; glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 31. Curd rice is one of the better rice-based choices for diabetic patients — the yogurt protein and fat slow glucose absorption. Portion control (150–200g) is still advisable. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Probiotics in yogurt (curd) support gut microbiome health linked to immune modulation; calcium supports bone health; ginger and mustard seeds are anti-inflammatory. Excellent choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the probiotic content of fresh curd supports immune regulation and the anti-inflammatory spices in the tempering provide additional benefit. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt/milk) — not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 20mg per bowl (250g). Vitamin B12 present from dairy. Probiotic content depends on whether yogurt is freshly set and live cultures are active — commercial yogurt may have reduced probiotic activity.

Reduce added salt during preparation. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~58) due to the yogurt protein and fat slowing starch digestion; glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 31. Curd rice is one of the better rice-based choices for diabetic patients — the yogurt protein and fat slow glucose absorption. Portion control (150–200g) is still advisable. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Probiotics in yogurt (curd) support gut microbiome health linked to immune modulation; calcium supports bone health; ginger and mustard seeds are anti-inflammatory. Excellent choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the probiotic content of fresh curd supports immune regulation and the anti-inflammatory spices in the tempering provide additional benefit. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt/milk) — not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 20mg per bowl (250g). Vitamin B12 present from dairy. Probiotic content depends on whether yogurt is freshly set and live cultures are active — commercial yogurt may have reduced probiotic activity.

This is **Aviyal**. Aviyal is a traditional Kerala Sadya (feast) dish — a semi-dry, richly fragrant curry made from a colourful medley of vegetables (raw banana, drumstick, yam, carrot, beans, raw mango) coated in a freshly ground paste of coconut, green chilli, and cumin. The vegetables are cooked until just tender, then finished with a generous ladle of whisked plain yogurt (which thickens the sauce) and drizzled with raw coconut oil and fresh curry leaves. The yogurt adds a characteristic mild sourness. A traditional banana-leaf meal (Onam Sadya) is incomplete without aviyal. In Singapore, aviyal is served at Kerala and South Indian restaurants. Per 100g: 118.0 kcal, 2.2g protein, 7.5g fat, 12.5g carbohydrates, 3.5g dietary fibre, 215.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 48 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 9.0. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from mixed vegetable preparation with coconut; high fibre and fat content gives low-moderate GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 323mg per serving (150g) — moderate; high potassium content (~428mg per serving) from vegetables is beneficial for blood pressure. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — the high potassium-to-sodium ratio from the diverse vegetables supports blood pressure management. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Moderate GI (~48); glycaemic load per 150g serving is approximately 9 — low. Dietary fibre (5.3g per serving) and coconut fat slow glucose absorption. Highly suitable for diabetic patients — moderate GI, high fibre, and diverse vegetable content. Aviyal provides excellent nutrient density with a low glycaemic load. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Drumstick (moringa) provides exceptional iron, calcium, and anti-inflammatory compounds; turmeric provides curcumin; the diverse vegetable content provides antioxidants. Aviyal is highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the combination of moringa, turmeric, cumin, and fresh vegetables makes it one of the most nutrient-dense and anti-inflammatory dishes in South Indian cuisine. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt) and coconut (tree nut). Cholesterol approximately 3mg per 150g serving from yogurt. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as the trace B12 from yogurt is negligible in this proportion. Vitamin C is notably high due to drumstick pods and raw mango content. The specific vegetable mix greatly affects nutrient profile.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Keerai Masiyal**. Keerai Masiyal is a traditional South Indian Tamil dish of cooked, mashed spinach (keerai) combined with soft toor dal or moong dal into a thick, smooth, dark-green mash. The dish is finished with a hot ghee tempering of mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and garlic, which adds fragrance and depth. It is one of the most iron- and folate-dense side dishes in South Indian cuisine. Eaten as a side with plain rice and rasam as part of a Tamil home meal. High in calcium, iron, vitamin A, and folate — nutritionally exceptional despite its simple preparation. Per 100g: 88.0 kcal, 4.5g protein, 3.5g fat, 11.2g carbohydrates, 3.2g dietary fibre, 185.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 32 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 5.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from cooked spinach and lentil mixture; very high fibre and protein content gives very low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 278mg per serving (150g) — moderate; the very high potassium (~480mg per serving) from spinach and dal provides excellent blood pressure benefit. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — the potassium-sodium ratio is highly favourable. Spinach is one of the best dietary sources of potassium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~32); glycaemic load per 150g serving is approximately 5 — very low. High fibre, protein, and magnesium from spinach and dal are beneficial for insulin sensitivity. Highly recommended for diabetic patients — very low GI and GL, high in fibre and magnesium which improves insulin sensitivity. Regular consumption of leafy greens is strongly associated with reduced diabetes risk. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Spinach is one of the richest sources of folate (~88mcg DFE per 100g) and vitamin A in South Indian cuisine; turmeric provides curcumin; iron is a concern for patients with active inflammation. Highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the folate content supports cell repair, vitamin A supports immune function, and turmeric provides anti-inflammatory benefits. The ghee tempering provides fat-soluble vitamin absorption. Note: Contains ghee (dairy) — not vegan or dairy-free when made with ghee; vegan when made with oil. Cholesterol approximately 7.5mg per serving (150g) when made with ghee. Oxalate content of spinach may reduce calcium bioavailability — calcium from other foods in the meal is better absorbed. Iron is non-heme iron (plant source) — pair with vitamin C-rich foods to enhance absorption.

This is **Peanut Sundal**. Peanut Sundal is a classic South Indian snack from Tamil Nadu — raw peanuts (groundnuts) soaked and boiled until tender, then dry-tempered in oil with mustard seeds, urad dal, curry leaves, dried red chilli, and green chilli, finished with freshly grated coconut. The peanuts retain their natural nutty flavour and are moist but not swimming in oil — a dry, protein-rich snack. Sundal is a traditional Navratri festival offering and temple prasad in Tamil Nadu. In Singapore, peanut sundal is served at South Indian restaurants and tiffin counters as a starter or snack. Per 100g: 252.0 kcal, 10.5g protein, 17.5g fat, 16.5g carbohydrates, 5.2g dietary fibre, 215.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 215mg per 100g serving — moderate; high potassium (385mg/100g) from peanuts is beneficial for blood pressure. Peanuts are also a source of magnesium which supports vascular health. Suitable for hypertensive patients at standard serving sizes. Peanuts contain unsaturated fats that are beneficial for cardiovascular health. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Expected very low GI (peanuts have a GI of ~14); high in protein (10.5g/100g), fat (17.5g/100g), and fibre (5.2g/100g) which collectively minimise the glycaemic response. Excellent snack for diabetic patients — peanuts have one of the lowest GI values of all foods. Peanut sundal provides sustained energy without a blood glucose spike. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Peanuts are rich in vitamin E (4.5mg/100g) and folate — both important for immune function and anti-inflammatory pathways; magnesium supports bone health. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the vitamin E and folate content supports immune modulation. Moderate consumption is advisable due to the peanut allergen potential in affected individuals. Note: Contains peanut and coconut (tree nut) allergens. Vegan and gluten-free. Cholesterol is 0.0. GI and GL are null — no testing data available but expected very low based on peanut composition. High caloric density per 100g from fat content; standard serving is 100g.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Dahi Vada**. Dahi Vada is a popular Indian chaat dish — urad dal fritters (vadas) that are first deep-fried, then soaked in water to remove excess oil and soften, then submerged in chilled sweetened plain yogurt and finished with a generous topping of dark tamarind chutney, vibrant green mint-coriander chutney, chaat masala, roasted cumin, and chilli powder. Served cold, it is simultaneously creamy, tangy, sweet, and spiced. The deep-fried vada absorbs the yogurt to become soft and spongy in contrast to the crispy fresh medu vada. Rich in protein and calcium from both lentil and dairy sources. Per 100g: 142.0 kcal, 5.8g protein, 5.5g fat, 18.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 298.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 52 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 19.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from urad dal fritter in yogurt; the yogurt protein and fat, combined with lentil structure, moderates the GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 596mg per 2-piece serving (200g) — elevated; chutney toppings (especially tamarind chutney) and chaat masala are the primary sodium contributors. Patients with hypertension should limit dahi vada to 1 piece and request reduced-salt chutneys. The yogurt component provides calcium which is beneficial for blood pressure. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~52); glycaemic load per serving is approximately 19. The sweetened yogurt contributes 6.5g sugar per 100g which is a concern for diabetic patients. Diabetic patients should request dahi vada with unsweetened yogurt (no added sugar) and minimal tamarind chutney. Limit to 1 piece. The lentil base provides good protein content. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Urad dal is an excellent source of folate and protein; yogurt provides probiotics and calcium; cumin and chilli have anti-inflammatory properties. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis when made with unsweetened yogurt. The probiotic content of fresh yogurt and the anti-inflammatory spice toppings are beneficial. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt) and is not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 17mg per 2-piece serving (200g) from yogurt. Vitamin B12 present from dairy. Sugar content is elevated due to sweetened yogurt — request unsweetened for diabetic patients.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Palak Paneer**. Palak Paneer is one of India's most beloved vegetarian curries — soft, fresh paneer (Indian cottage cheese) cubes nestled in a thick, velvety, bright-green sauce made from blanched and puréed fresh spinach, cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, cumin, garam masala, and finished with a splash of cream or yogurt. The spinach sauce is smooth and intensely green, with the paneer providing a mild, creamy contrast. Nutritionally exceptional — very low GI, high calcium, high vitamin A, high folate, and rich in plant iron. Available at virtually all North Indian restaurants in Singapore. Per 100g: 172.0 kcal, 7.5g protein, 13.2g fat, 7.0g carbohydrates, 2.2g dietary fibre, 312.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 22 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 3.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate curry; paneer and spinach combination gives very low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 624mg per bowl (200g) — elevated; restaurant palak paneer tends to be high in salt and cream; home cooking allows control. Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparations and minimal cream. The high calcium (185mg/100g) and potassium content from spinach and paneer are beneficial for blood pressure management. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~22) and very low carbohydrate content (7g/100g); glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 3 — negligible. High protein and fat slow any glucose absorption. Excellent choice for diabetic patients — palak paneer has one of the lowest glycaemic loads of all Indian restaurant dishes. The high protein (15g per bowl) supports satiety and blood sugar control. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Spinach provides exceptional folate (88mcg DFE/100g) and vitamin A (320mcg RAE/100g); turmeric provides curcumin; paneer provides calcium for bone health. Highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the combination of spinach's anti-inflammatory micronutrients, turmeric's curcumin, and paneer's calcium makes this one of the most beneficial curries for bone and joint health. Note: Contains dairy (paneer and cream/yogurt) — not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 64mg per bowl (200g). Vitamin B12 present from dairy. High saturated fat from paneer and cream — patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size. Spinach oxalate reduces calcium bioavailability from spinach itself, but paneer calcium is well-absorbed.

This is **Palak Paneer**. Palak Paneer is one of India's most beloved vegetarian curries — soft, fresh paneer (Indian cottage cheese) cubes nestled in a thick, velvety, bright-green sauce made from blanched and puréed fresh spinach, cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, cumin, garam masala, and finished with a splash of cream or yogurt. The spinach sauce is smooth and intensely green, with the paneer providing a mild, creamy contrast. Nutritionally exceptional — very low GI, high calcium, high vitamin A, high folate, and rich in plant iron. Available at virtually all North Indian restaurants in Singapore. Per 100g: 172.0 kcal, 7.5g protein, 13.2g fat, 7.0g carbohydrates, 2.2g dietary fibre, 312.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 22 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 3.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate curry; paneer and spinach combination gives very low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 624mg per bowl (200g) — elevated; restaurant palak paneer tends to be high in salt and cream; home cooking allows control. Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparations and minimal cream. The high calcium (185mg/100g) and potassium content from spinach and paneer are beneficial for blood pressure management. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~22) and very low carbohydrate content (7g/100g); glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 3 — negligible. High protein and fat slow any glucose absorption. Excellent choice for diabetic patients — palak paneer has one of the lowest glycaemic loads of all Indian restaurant dishes. The high protein (15g per bowl) supports satiety and blood sugar control. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Spinach provides exceptional folate (88mcg DFE/100g) and vitamin A (320mcg RAE/100g); turmeric provides curcumin; paneer provides calcium for bone health. Highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the combination of spinach's anti-inflammatory micronutrients, turmeric's curcumin, and paneer's calcium makes this one of the most beneficial curries for bone and joint health. Note: Contains dairy (paneer and cream/yogurt) — not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 64mg per bowl (200g). Vitamin B12 present from dairy. High saturated fat from paneer and cream — patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size. Spinach oxalate reduces calcium bioavailability from spinach itself, but paneer calcium is well-absorbed.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Paneer Butter Masala**. Paneer Butter Masala (also called Paneer Makhani) is one of North India's most celebrated curries — soft fresh paneer cubes simmered in a luxuriously smooth, mildly spiced, orange-red sauce made from puréed tomatoes, fried onion, cashew paste, generous butter, and fresh cream. The sauce is always blended smooth and has a distinctly silky, velvety texture. A signature note of kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves) gives the dish its characteristic aroma. Rich in calcium and vitamin A but high in saturated fat and sodium, making it an indulgence dish best consumed in moderation. Per 100g: 212.0 kcal, 7.2g protein, 16.5g fat, 9.5g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 358.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 28 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 5.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from high-fat, high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate tomato-dairy curry; the cashew fat and cream significantly lower GI. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 716mg per bowl (200g) — high; restaurant paneer butter masala is typically high in butter, cream, and salt. Calcium from paneer and dairy is beneficial. Patients with hypertension should limit to a half portion and choose reduced-butter preparations. Home cooking allows significant sodium reduction. The calcium benefit does not outweigh the sodium burden at restaurant serving levels. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Low GI (~28); glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 5. Very low in carbohydrates and very high in fat and protein which prevent blood glucose spikes. Paneer butter masala is acceptable for diabetic patients in moderate portions — the high fat content (16.5g/100g) does not cause a glycaemic spike. However, the high saturated fat content warrants general moderation. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. High saturated fat (9.2g/100g) from butter, cream, and paneer may promote inflammation if consumed in excess; tomato lycopene and kasuri methi have modest anti-inflammatory properties. Consume in moderation — limit to 1 bowl twice weekly. The high saturated fat content from butter and cream may exacerbate inflammation in active disease. Reduced-cream preparations are preferred. Note: Contains dairy (paneer, butter, cream) and tree nut (cashew) allergens. Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 104mg per bowl (200g). Vitamin B12 present from dairy. The trans fat (0.1g/100g) is from dairy natural trans fat (conjugated linoleic acid from butter), not industrial trans fat.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chana Masala**. Chana Masala is a North Indian and Punjabi staple — plump, pressure-cooked white chickpeas (kabuli chana) simmered in a robust, tangy, slightly dry tomato-onion gravy fragrant with cumin, coriander, garam masala, and a special chana masala spice blend that typically includes amchur (dried mango powder) and pomegranate seeds. The curry is drier and more intensely spiced than many other Indian curries. It is one of the most nutritionally complete legume dishes — very high in fibre, protein, folate, and potassium, with a very low GI. Paired with bhature or puri for the classic Chole Bhature combination, or eaten with rice. Per 100g: 148.0 kcal, 7.2g protein, 4.5g fat, 21.5g carbohydrates, 6.5g dietary fibre, 285.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 28 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 12.0. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — canned chickpeas in spiced curry GI approximately 28; the very high fibre (6.5g/100g) and protein content of chickpeas gives a very low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 570mg per bowl (200g) — moderate; the very high potassium (596mg per bowl) and magnesium from chickpeas are beneficial for blood pressure regulation. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — chickpeas are one of the most potassium-rich legumes; the potassium-to-sodium ratio is highly favourable. Reduce added salt during cooking. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~28); glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 12. The resistant starch and fibre in chickpeas significantly slow glucose absorption. Chana masala is highly recommended for diabetic patients — chickpeas are among the most diabetes-friendly carbohydrates available. Regular chickpea consumption is associated with improved long-term glycaemic control. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Chickpeas are rich in folate (52mcg DFE/100g), magnesium, and protein; turmeric and cumin provide anti-inflammatory compounds; amchur provides vitamin C for iron absorption. Highly suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the high folate, magnesium, and protein content supports immune function and muscle health. Regular legume consumption is associated with reduced inflammatory markers. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Cholesterol is 0.0. Exceptionally high fibre (6.5g/100g) — one of the highest fibre dishes in Indian cuisine. The amchur (dried mango powder) contributes to the tangy flavour and provides small amounts of vitamin C.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Dal Makhani**. Dal Makhani is one of North India's most iconic slow-cooked dishes — whole black urad dal and red kidney beans (rajma) that have been soaked overnight and then simmered for 8–12 hours with tomato, garlic, ginger, and aromatic spices, then finished with butter and cream. The extended cooking develops an extraordinarily thick, velvety, dark-coloured curry with a rich, smoky, deeply earthy flavour. Kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves) add a characteristic bittersweet finish. Dal makhani is available at virtually every North Indian restaurant in Singapore and is a vegetarian classic with impressive nutritional credentials (high fibre, high protein, very low GI) despite its indulgent dairy additions. Per 100g: 162.0 kcal, 7.5g protein, 8.8g fat, 17.5g carbohydrates, 5.5g dietary fibre, 325.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 30 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 10.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from whole black urad dal and kidney beans; whole legumes with butter and cream give a very low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 650mg per bowl (200g) — elevated for restaurant preparation; butter and cream add to the cardiovascular burden. Patients with hypertension should choose home-cooked versions with reduced butter and salt. The high potassium (550mg per bowl) and magnesium from lentils partially offset the sodium impact. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low GI (~30); glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 10.5. The whole legume structure and high fibre (5.5g/100g) give excellent glycaemic control. Dal makhani is an excellent choice for diabetic patients in moderate portions — the whole lentils and beans provide sustained energy. The butter and cream content does not raise blood glucose. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. High saturated fat from butter and cream (4.5g/100g) may be pro-inflammatory in excess; whole urad dal and kidney beans are excellent sources of folate, magnesium, and plant protein. Consume in moderation — the legume base is highly beneficial but the high saturated fat from butter and cream warrants caution for patients with active rheumatoid arthritis. Reduced-fat preparations are preferred. Note: Contains dairy (butter, cream) — not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 36mg per bowl (200g) from butter and cream. Vitamin B12 present at trace levels from dairy. The long slow-cooking of whole legumes breaks down antinutrients (phytates, tannins) and significantly improves zinc and iron bioavailability.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chicken Tikka**. Chicken Tikka is one of the most beloved Indian starter dishes — boneless pieces of chicken (breast or thigh) marinated in a yogurt and spice marinade (ginger-garlic paste, red chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, and lemon) for several hours, then skewered and cooked at high heat in a tandoor clay oven until charred at the edges and cooked through. Served without sauce as a dry starter, garnished with sliced onion, lemon wedges, and mint chutney. Very high in protein, low in carbohydrates, and rich in selenium and B vitamins. Per 100g: 198.0 kcal, 22.5g protein, 10.5g fat, 3.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 425.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 850mg per serving (200g) — elevated; the marinade and dry-rub spice blend contain significant salt; restaurant versions are often high sodium. Patients with hypertension should limit to half a serving (100g) or request reduced-salt preparation. The chicken itself is an excellent source of potassium and protein for cardiovascular health. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Virtually no carbohydrates; GI testing not applicable. High protein (22.5g/100g) and moderate fat support satiety and blood glucose stability. Excellent choice for diabetic patients — chicken tikka provides high protein with negligible glycaemic impact. Pair with salad or raita instead of naan or paratha to maintain the low-carbohydrate profile. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Excellent source of selenium (28mcg/100g) which is a key antioxidant for immune function; turmeric in the spice blend provides curcumin; very high niacin and B6. Chicken tikka is one of the better lean protein options for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The spice blend provides anti-inflammatory compounds. Selenium supports glutathione peroxidase — a key antioxidant enzyme. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt in marinade at trace levels after cooking). Not suitable for vegans or strict dairy-free diets. Cholesterol approximately 156mg per serving (200g). GI and GL are null — carbohydrate content is negligible. Grilling over direct flame may create heterocyclic amines (HCAs) — avoid charred portions to minimise carcinogen exposure.

Per 100g: 198.0 kcal, 22.5g protein, 10.5g fat, 3.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 425.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 850mg per serving (200g) — elevated; the marinade and dry-rub spice blend contain significant salt; restaurant versions are often high sodium. Patients with hypertension should limit to half a serving (100g) or request reduced-salt preparation. The chicken itself is an excellent source of potassium and protein for cardiovascular health. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Virtually no carbohydrates; GI testing not applicable. High protein (22.5g/100g) and moderate fat support satiety and blood glucose stability. Excellent choice for diabetic patients — chicken tikka provides high protein with negligible glycaemic impact. Pair with salad or raita instead of naan or paratha to maintain the low-carbohydrate profile. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Excellent source of selenium (28mcg/100g) which is a key antioxidant for immune function; turmeric in the spice blend provides curcumin; very high niacin and B6. Chicken tikka is one of the better lean protein options for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The spice blend provides anti-inflammatory compounds. Selenium supports glutathione peroxidase — a key antioxidant enzyme. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt in marinade at trace levels after cooking). Not suitable for vegans or strict dairy-free diets. Cholesterol approximately 156mg per serving (200g). GI and GL are null — carbohydrate content is negligible. Grilling over direct flame may create heterocyclic amines (HCAs) — avoid charred portions to minimise carcinogen exposure.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chicken Korma**. Chicken Korma is one of India's most celebrated Mughlai curries — chicken pieces braised in a rich, mildly spiced, pale-golden sauce made from yogurt, fried onion paste, cashew or almond paste, cream, and aromatic whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf). It is distinctly and intentionally mild in chilli heat — the flavour is aromatic, slightly sweet, and rich with nut and dairy fat. Often finished with saffron. A popular choice for those who prefer mild curries, and a classic at Indian restaurant buffets in Singapore. Per 100g: 192.0 kcal, 14.5g protein, 14.2g fat, 5.0g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 318.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 795mg per bowl (250g) — elevated for restaurant preparation; restaurant korma may contain additional salt and cream. Patients with hypertension should limit to a smaller portion (150g) or request reduced-salt preparation. Home cooking with reduced cream and salt is strongly preferred. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low carbohydrates (5g/100g); GI testing not applicable. High protein and fat provide excellent satiety without a blood glucose spike. Chicken korma is acceptable for diabetic patients — the very low carbohydrate content does not raise blood glucose. However, the high saturated fat from cream warrants general moderation. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. High saturated fat from cream (5.5g/100g) may promote inflammation if consumed frequently; cardamom and cinnamon have modest anti-inflammatory properties. Consume in moderation — the high saturated fat content from cream warrants caution for patients with active rheumatoid arthritis. Choose reduced-cream preparations. The aromatic spices provide beneficial anti-inflammatory compounds. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt, cream) and tree nuts (cashew/almond). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 155mg per bowl (250g). GI and GL are null — insufficient carbohydrate. Restaurant korma tends to use more cream than home cooking — values may be higher in restaurant servings.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chapati**. Chapati (also called Roti or Phulka) is the most widely consumed bread in India — a thin, unleavened flatbread made only from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and salt, hand-rolled into thin rounds and dry-cooked on a flat cast-iron griddle (tawa) until brown spots appear, then briefly held over a direct flame to puff up with steam. No fat is added during cooking, making it one of the lowest-fat breads. Often brushed with a small amount of ghee after cooking for flavour. High in fibre, B vitamins, and minerals from whole wheat. The nutritional standard-bearer among Indian breads. Per 100g: 238.0 kcal, 8.2g protein, 1.8g fat, 48.5g carbohydrates, 5.5g dietary fibre, 195.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 52 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 30.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — whole wheat chapati GI approximately 52; the whole wheat flour and high fibre moderate the GI vs white bread. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 234mg per 2-chapati serving (120g) — low; chapati is one of the lower-sodium breads available. High magnesium and potassium from whole wheat support blood pressure. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — low in sodium, high in magnesium and potassium. Chapati is significantly better than white bread or naan for blood pressure management. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~52) due to whole wheat fibre; glycaemic load per 2-chapati serving (120g) is approximately 30. Significantly lower GI than white bread, plain naan, or poori. Chapati is the preferred bread choice for diabetic patients in Indian cuisine — the whole wheat content gives a lower GI than refined flour breads. Limit to 2 chapatis per meal and pair with high-protein sides. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Whole wheat is an excellent source of selenium (28mcg/100g) and magnesium; B vitamins thiamine and niacin are important for energy metabolism and inflammation control. Chapati is suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis who do not have gluten intolerance. The high selenium and magnesium content of whole wheat are beneficial for immune function and bone health. Note: Contains gluten (whole wheat) — not suitable for coeliac or gluten-sensitive patients. Vegan in standard preparation (no fat); vegetarian when brushed with ghee (adds ~15 kcal and trace dairy per chapati). Cholesterol is 0.0 for plain preparation. If ghee is added post-cooking, add approximately 25–35 kcal and 3g fat per chapati.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Plain Naan**. Plain Naan is a leavened flatbread — synonymous with North Indian restaurant dining — made from refined white flour (maida) leavened with yogurt and yeast or baking powder, stretched by hand into a teardrop shape and baked at high heat directly on the inner wall of a clay tandoor oven. The surface develops beautiful golden-brown blisters while the interior remains soft and chewy. After baking, it is brushed with butter or ghee. Compared to chapati, naan is higher in calories, fat, sodium, and has a higher GI due to the refined flour, but has a superior soft texture that makes it a popular restaurant choice. Per 100g: 285.0 kcal, 8.0g protein, 6.8g fat, 49.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 485.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 71 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 42.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white flour naan GI approximately 71; refined flour and minimal fibre give a high GI. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 582mg per piece (120g) — elevated; naan contains yogurt, salt in dough, and butter brushing, all contributing to high sodium. Patients with hypertension should limit naan to half a piece or choose chapati instead. Naan has more than twice the sodium of chapati at equivalent serving sizes. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. High GI (~71) from refined flour; glycaemic load per full piece (120g) is approximately 42 — high. Minimal fibre provides no protection against blood glucose spikes. Diabetic patients should limit to half a piece or choose chapati (whole wheat) instead, which has a significantly lower GI (~52). Pair with high-protein curries to moderate the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Refined flour is neutral for inflammation at standard serving levels; butter provides small amounts of fat-soluble vitamins. Suitable in moderation for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. No specific anti-inflammatory benefit from plain naan. Whole wheat alternatives (chapati, roti) are nutritionally superior. Note: Contains gluten (refined wheat) and dairy (yogurt, butter). Not vegan or suitable for gluten-sensitive patients. Cholesterol approximately 14mg per piece (120g) from butter/yogurt. Low fibre (1.8g/100g) compared to chapati (5.5g/100g). Garlic naan and butter naan have higher fat and calorie content.

Aloo Paratha is the quintessential Punjabi breakfast and comfort food — a generous whole wheat flatbread stuffed with a spiced mashed potato filling (cumin, green chilli, coriander, amchur, garam masala) and cooked on a hot griddle with ghee until golden and slightly crispy on both sides. Rich, filling, and fragrant. Served with a bowl of cold fresh yogurt and a tangy pickle (achar). The combination of whole wheat dough and potato filling creates a complete, satisfying meal. In Singapore, aloo paratha is available at North Indian restaurants and some hawker stalls. Per 100g: 272.0 kcal, 6.2g protein, 10.5g fat, 40.5g carbohydrates, 3.8g dietary fibre, 312.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 66 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 34.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from whole wheat paratha with potato filling; the ghee and potato together give a moderate GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 406mg per piece (130g) — moderate; the ghee in cooking adds saturated fat but minimal sodium. Suitable in moderation for hypertensive patients. The high potassium from potato (370mg per paratha) partially offsets the sodium. Request oil-based cooking instead of ghee to reduce saturated fat. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Moderate GI (~66); glycaemic load per piece (130g) is approximately 35 — high. The potato filling and whole wheat dough both contribute carbohydrates. Diabetic patients should limit to half a piece and pair with a high-protein side (yogurt, dal) to moderate the glycaemic response. The ghee slightly lowers the GI but the total glycaemic load remains substantial. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Ghee provides butyrate and fat-soluble vitamins; whole wheat provides selenium and B vitamins; potato provides vitamin B6 and vitamin C. Suitable in moderation for patients with rheumatoid arthritis who do not have gluten intolerance. The ghee and B vitamins provide some benefit. Nightshade-free diet followers should avoid (potato filling). Note: Contains gluten (wheat) and dairy (ghee). Not vegan or suitable for gluten-sensitive patients. Cholesterol approximately 23mg per piece (130g) from ghee. Vitamin C from potato and amchur. Some rheumatoid arthritis patients follow nightshade-free diets which would exclude the potato filling.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Poori**. Poori (or Puri) is a small, round, deep-fried flatbread made from whole wheat dough — when it hits the hot oil, it puffs dramatically into a hollow golden sphere due to steam trapped inside. Eaten immediately while still puffed, the exterior is crispy and the interior soft. Classically served with aloo bhaji (spiced potato), chana masala, or halwa for breakfast, or with chicken/mutton curry as a main meal. Despite being made from whole wheat, the deep-frying significantly increases calorie content. Vegan in standard preparation. Per 100g: 328.0 kcal, 7.5g protein, 16.5g fat, 42.0g carbohydrates, 4.2g dietary fibre, 185.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 66 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 33.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from whole wheat fried bread; oil absorption slightly lowers GI vs boiled wheat products but the high carbohydrate density creates a significant glycaemic load. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 222mg per 3-poori serving (120g) — low; poori is naturally low in sodium as no salt is typically added beyond a minimal amount. Poori is low in sodium and suitable for hypertensive patients from a sodium standpoint. The main concern is the high fat content from deep frying rather than sodium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Moderate GI (~66) but very high caloric density from deep frying; glycaemic load per 3-poori serving (120g) is approximately 33. Diabetic patients should limit poori to 1–2 pieces on special occasions. The deep-frying dramatically increases calorie density vs chapati. Choose chapati or roti as the daily bread option. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. Deep-frying in vegetable oil introduces high levels of polyunsaturated omega-6 fatty acids which, in excess, may promote inflammation; the oil quality used for frying is critical. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit poori consumption. The deep-frying method introduces pro-inflammatory omega-6 fats. Occasional consumption is acceptable but not recommended as a regular choice. Note: Contains gluten (wheat). Vegan in standard preparation (water and salt only). Cholesterol is 0.0. The trans fat content depends on the type of oil used for frying — repeatedly reused oil may contain trans fats; fresh vegetable oil does not. High caloric density (328 kcal/100g) is driven by the absorbed frying oil (~16.5g fat/100g).

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Murtabak (Mutton)**. Murtabak (Mutton) is one of Singapore's most iconic Indian Muslim street foods — a large, thin white flour pancake that is folded around a spiced mixture of minced mutton, beaten egg, and diced onion, then pressed flat and pan-fried in oil on a large flat griddle until golden and crispy on both sides. Cut into squares and served with a curry dipping sauce and a small bowl of sliced onion pickled in vinegar. Available at dedicated murtabak stalls in Arab Street / Kampong Glam and at Indian Muslim restaurants across Singapore. A flavourful, filling, high-calorie dish. Per 100g: 258.0 kcal, 11.5g protein, 14.5g fat, 22.5g carbohydrates, 1.2g dietary fibre, 568.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 52 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 35.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from white flour pancake with meat and egg filling; the high protein and fat content moderates the GI of the white flour dough. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1704mg per full piece (300g) — very high; the dipping curry sauce, seasoned filling, and frying oil all contribute significantly to sodium. Patients with hypertension should limit to a half piece (150g) and avoid the dipping curry sauce or ask for a reduced-salt version. Murtabak is one of the highest-sodium single-serve Indian dishes. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~52) due to the protein and fat in the filling; glycaemic load per full piece (300g) is approximately 35. The high total calories (774 kcal per piece) warrant overall portion control. Diabetic patients should limit to a half piece. The egg and mutton filling provide protein which moderates the glycaemic response. Avoid the sweetened dipping sauce variant. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. Mutton is a red meat high in arachidonic acid which can promote inflammation; however it provides excellent zinc (3.5mg/100g) and vitamin B12 which support immune function. Consume in moderation — limit to monthly. The zinc and B12 content is beneficial for immune function but the red meat (mutton) and frying method may exacerbate inflammation with regular consumption. Note: Contains gluten (wheat flour) and eggs. Halal-certified preparation uses mutton (goat). Cholesterol approximately 264mg per full piece (300g) from eggs and mutton. Vitamin B12 is notably high from mutton content. The dipping curry sauce sodium is included in HPB data. Chicken murtabak (a common variant) has lower zinc and B12 but lower saturated fat.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). This is **Jeera Rice**. Jeera Rice is a simple, aromatic North Indian rice dish — long-grain aged basmati rice cooked by the absorption method to achieve individual, separate, fluffy grains, then finished with a quick tempering of ghee and whole cumin seeds (jeera) that are crackled in hot ghee until fragrant. The result is a perfectly seasoned rice with a subtle nutty-earthy cumin fragrance. Served as the plain rice accompaniment to Indian curries at virtually every North Indian restaurant. Basmati rice has a lower GI than regular white rice, making it a slightly better choice for blood glucose management. Per 100g: 162.0 kcal, 3.5g protein, 3.2g fat, 31.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 145.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 58 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 36.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — cooked basmati rice GI approximately 58; the ghee and cumin tempering slightly lower GI vs plain boiled rice. Basmati has a lower GI than regular long-grain white rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 290mg per bowl (200g) — low to moderate. Jeera rice is naturally low in sodium. Suitable for hypertensive patients. One of the lower-sodium rice preparations. Cumin seeds may have mild blood pressure-lowering effects in regular dietary quantities. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~58) — lower than regular white rice; glycaemic load per bowl (200g) is approximately 37. The basmati rice GI is meaningfully lower than standard white rice (~73). Jeera rice is a better choice than regular rice for diabetic patients due to basmati's lower GI. Limit to 150g portions and pair with high-protein, high-fibre curries to further moderate the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Cumin has documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties; ghee provides butyrate and fat-soluble vitamins. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the cumin provides anti-inflammatory compounds and the ghee provides beneficial fat-soluble nutrients. Note: Contains dairy (ghee) — not vegan or dairy-free when made with ghee. Vegan when made with oil. Cholesterol approximately 15mg per bowl (200g) when made with ghee; 0.0 when made with oil. Cumin seeds at the standard proportion (~0.5g per 100g cooked rice) contribute negligible additional calories.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Khichdi**. Khichdi is India's original comfort food — a simple, soothing one-pot porridge of white rice and split yellow moong dal cooked together with water, turmeric, cumin seeds, and a small amount of ghee until the rice and dal lose their individual structure and merge into a smooth, slightly pourable, pale-yellow porridge. It is the most widely prescribed therapeutic food in Indian dietary practice — recommended for convalescence, upset stomachs, fevers, and recovery. Nutritionally, it provides a complete amino acid profile from the rice-dal combination, with very low sodium and easy digestibility. Plain khichdi is deeply humble in flavour and deeply healing in effect. Per 100g: 92.0 kcal, 3.2g protein, 1.8g fat, 17.5g carbohydrates, 1.0g dietary fibre, 142.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 65 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 34.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from soft-cooked rice and moong dal mixture; the moong dal lowers GI vs plain cooked rice; the very soft porridge consistency raises GI slightly vs firm-cooked rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 426mg per bowl (300g) — moderate; khichdi is naturally low in sodium and can be prepared with minimal salt. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — one of the lowest-sodium complete meals in Indian cuisine. The high water content and mild preparation make it ideal for patients requiring bland, easy-to-digest food. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~65); glycaemic load per bowl (300g) is approximately 34. The moong dal provides protein and fibre that meaningfully lowers the GI compared to plain rice porridge. Khichdi is suitable for diabetic patients as a comfort/convalescent meal — limit to 200g portions. The moong dal content provides protein and fibre that moderate the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Moong dal is the easiest legume to digest and has very low purine content; turmeric provides curcumin; ghee provides butyrate. Traditionally considered the most anti-inflammatory and gut-healing Indian food. Khichdi is excellent for patients with rheumatoid arthritis, particularly during flares — it is extremely easy to digest, anti-inflammatory (turmeric, ghee), and nutritionally complete as a light meal. Note: Contains dairy (ghee) — not vegan or dairy-free when made with ghee. Vegan when made with oil. Cholesterol approximately 12mg per bowl (300g) when made with ghee. Khichdi prepared with more dal has a lower GI and higher protein content. Vegetable additions (spinach, peas, ginger) significantly improve the micronutrient profile.

This is **Khichdi**. Khichdi is India's original comfort food — a simple, soothing one-pot porridge of white rice and split yellow moong dal cooked together with water, turmeric, cumin seeds, and a small amount of ghee until the rice and dal lose their individual structure and merge into a smooth, slightly pourable, pale-yellow porridge. It is the most widely prescribed therapeutic food in Indian dietary practice — recommended for convalescence, upset stomachs, fevers, and recovery. Nutritionally, it provides a complete amino acid profile from the rice-dal combination, with very low sodium and easy digestibility. Plain khichdi is deeply humble in flavour and deeply healing in effect. Per 100g: 92.0 kcal, 3.2g protein, 1.8g fat, 17.5g carbohydrates, 1.0g dietary fibre, 142.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 65 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 34.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from soft-cooked rice and moong dal mixture; the moong dal lowers GI vs plain cooked rice; the very soft porridge consistency raises GI slightly vs firm-cooked rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 426mg per bowl (300g) — moderate; khichdi is naturally low in sodium and can be prepared with minimal salt. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — one of the lowest-sodium complete meals in Indian cuisine. The high water content and mild preparation make it ideal for patients requiring bland, easy-to-digest food. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~65); glycaemic load per bowl (300g) is approximately 34. The moong dal provides protein and fibre that meaningfully lowers the GI compared to plain rice porridge. Khichdi is suitable for diabetic patients as a comfort/convalescent meal — limit to 200g portions. The moong dal content provides protein and fibre that moderate the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Moong dal is the easiest legume to digest and has very low purine content; turmeric provides curcumin; ghee provides butyrate. Traditionally considered the most anti-inflammatory and gut-healing Indian food. Khichdi is excellent for patients with rheumatoid arthritis, particularly during flares — it is extremely easy to digest, anti-inflammatory (turmeric, ghee), and nutritionally complete as a light meal. Note: Contains dairy (ghee) — not vegan or dairy-free when made with ghee. Vegan when made with oil. Cholesterol approximately 12mg per bowl (300g) when made with ghee. Khichdi prepared with more dal has a lower GI and higher protein content. Vegetable additions (spinach, peas, ginger) significantly improve the micronutrient profile.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Pani Puri**. Pani Puri (Gol Gappa) is the most exuberant Indian street food experience — small, round, hollow crispy spheres made from semolina or wheat flour, deep-fried until they puff up like tiny balloons. Each puri is cracked open with a thumb, stuffed with a spiced filling of mashed boiled potato and chickpeas (or sprouted moong), then dramatically flooded with spiced cold green tamarind water (pani) made from mint, coriander, ginger, cumin, and tangy black salt. The entire puri is popped into the mouth in one go — an explosion of crispy, tangy, spicy, sour, and savoury. A beloved street food sensation in Singapore's Indian community. Per 100g: 188.0 kcal, 3.8g protein, 6.5g fat, 31.2g carbohydrates, 2.5g dietary fibre, 485.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 62 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 19.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from deep-fried semolina and potato filling with tamarind water; the filling and water add fibre and reduce GI vs plain fried semolina. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 485mg per 100g (standard 6-puri serving) — elevated; the black salt (kala namak) and spiced tamarind water are particularly high in sodium. Patients with hypertension should limit pani puri consumption and be cautious of the spiced tamarind water (pani) which is the primary sodium source. Street vendor preparations are typically very high in sodium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~62); glycaemic load per 100g serving is approximately 19. The potato filling and semolina puri both contribute carbohydrates. Diabetic patients should limit to 3–4 pieces and minimise the tamarind-sugar water. The chickpea filling provides protein and fibre that moderate the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Tamarind has anti-inflammatory properties; mint and coriander in the pani contain antioxidants; black salt provides sulfur compounds. Acceptable occasionally for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The spice blend in pani puri provides various anti-inflammatory herbs. Black salt (kala namak) is lower in sodium than regular salt. Note: Contains wheat/semolina gluten. Vegan in standard preparation. Cholesterol is 0.0. Hygiene is a significant concern for street food pani puri — the water (pani) must be made with clean water. Black salt (kala namak) is used in authentic preparations and contributes distinctive sulphurous flavour; it has a different mineral profile than regular table salt.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Bhel Puri**. Bhel Puri is a quintessential Mumbai street food — a cold, crunchy, sweet-sour-spicy chaat made from puffed rice (murmura), crispy fine noodles of fried chickpea flour (sev), diced raw onion, tomato, boiled potato, and two chutneys: a vibrant green mint-coriander chutney and a dark, sweet-sour tamarind chutney. Everything is tossed together fresh at the moment of serving to maintain crunchiness. Bhel puri requires no cooking — it is purely an assembly of ingredients. Light, fresh, and flavourful with contrasting textures. Vegan and relatively low in fat for a street food snack. Per 100g: 162.0 kcal, 3.5g protein, 4.8g fat, 28.5g carbohydrates, 2.2g dietary fibre, 395.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 68 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 29.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from puffed rice; puffed rice has a high GI but the potato, sev, and chutney mixture moderates the overall GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 593mg per plate (150g) — elevated; chaat masala, tamarind chutney, and sev all contribute to high sodium content. Patients with hypertension should limit bhel puri to half a plate and request reduced chutney and chaat masala. The raw vegetables provide potassium but the sodium burden from toppings is significant. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate-high GI (~68) from puffed rice; glycaemic load per plate is approximately 29. The raw onion, tomato, and potato provide some glycaemic moderation. Diabetic patients should limit to a small serving (75–100g) and minimise the sweet tamarind chutney which adds sugar. The raw vegetable content is beneficial. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Fresh raw vegetables (onion, tomato, coriander) and tamarind provide antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds; lemon juice provides vitamin C. Suitable occasionally for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The fresh vegetables and herbs provide antioxidants. Limit the sweet tamarind chutney for patients monitoring sugar intake. Note: Contains wheat gluten (sev is typically made from chickpea flour but most commercial sev contains wheat starch). Vegan in standard preparation. Cholesterol is 0.0. The glycaemic response depends heavily on the ratio of sweet tamarind chutney (high sugar) to green chutney (low sugar) used.
